---
title: "Does This Look Random to You?"
format:
  closeread-html:
    theme: powerball-docket.scss
    css: closeread-docket.css
    remove-header-space: true
    self-contained: true
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
  echo: false
---

<!--
PHASE 4 — REAL CLOSEREAD WIRING
=====================================
Structure carried forward from PDC's `04_funafuti_tuvalu_threshold.qmd`
per handoff instruction — retheme colors only, don't rebuild the
skeleton. What changed vs. the cold-read draft:
  - format switched from `html` to `closeread-html`
  - three panels now live inside ONE continuous :::{.cr-section},
    each as its own #cr-panelN sticky, matching PDC's single-section
    multi-sticky pattern (not three isolated static images)
  - micro-trigger text added between stickies so the sticky-swap has
    something to trigger on: "Exhibit A / B / C" + one clause each.
    This is navigation, not narration — it does not restate a panel's
    question or reveal, and it is NOT the still-open "verdict divider"
    decision (open item 4 in the handoff). If verdict dividers are
    resolved later, they'd likely replace or extend this text — this
    is deliberately minimal until that decision is made.
  - JS scroll-listener workaround carried verbatim from PDC (bottom of
    this file) — Closeread v1.0.1 + Scrollama init-timing bug fix.
  - coldread-extras.css replaced by closeread-docket.css, which keeps
    the non-cr-section blocks (standfirst/methodology/coda/sources/
    tools/licence/footer) unchanged and adds the retheme'd cr-section/
    sticky/trigger rules PDC's styles.css defines but the docket scss
    never needed until now.

STILL NOT RESOLVED BY THIS PASS (carried from handoff, unchanged):
  - Panel 2 ring treatment: v2 (thin gold ring) wired below. v2b
    (graphite ring / gold numeral only) is the live alternate — swap
    the image path in #cr-panel2 to compare.
  - Verdict dividers between panels: still open (item 4). The Exhibit
    A/B/C micro-triggers below are structural, not that decision.
  - Working title placeholder note from the handoff (series eyebrow
    "THE RANDOMNESS DOCKET") — unaffected by this pass either way.
-->

<div class="header-row">
  <div class="eyebrow">THE RANDOMNESS DOCKET</div>
</div>

# Does This Look Random to You?

<div class="standfirst">
Real Powerball drawings can make ordinary randomness look suspicious. This
piece treats three patterns as exhibits: consecutive numbers, repeated
numbers between drawings, and a number that seems overdue. Each one is
compared with what a fair random process predicts.
</div>

<!-- ============================================================ -->
<!-- CLOSEREAD SEQUENCE — Panels 1–3 as one continuous scroll      -->
<!-- ============================================================ -->

:::{.cr-section}

:::{#cr-panel1}
![](../outputs/panel_01_consecutive_v3_docket.png){fig-alt="Exhibit showing five Powerball balls from the February 20, 2019 drawing — 27, 49, 50, 51, 52 — with the observed rate of consecutive pairs (26.4%) compared to a fair-random simulation (26.3%)."}
:::

**Exhibit A.** A single Powerball drawing. @cr-panel1

**Exhibit B.** Two consecutive drawings. @cr-panel2

:::{#cr-panel2}
![](../outputs/panel_02_overlap_v2_docket.png){fig-alt="Exhibit showing two consecutive Powerball drawings, February 9 and February 11, 2026, sharing the numbers 6 and 48, with the observed rate of at least one shared number (32.0%) compared to the hypergeometric expectation (32.2%)."}
:::

**Exhibit C.** A number that hasn't appeared in weeks. @cr-panel3

:::{#cr-panel3}
![](../outputs/panel_03_droughts_v5_docket.png){fig-alt="Exhibit showing number 23's current 48-draw drought compared to the typical range of 46 to 87 draws for the most-overdue number, from a 5,000-replicate simulation."}
:::

:::

<hr class="rule">

<!-- ============================================================ -->
<!-- MECHANISM — background text, not its own panel (per brief)   -->
<!-- Copy unchanged from Phase 3 cold-read.                        -->
<!-- ============================================================ -->

<div class="methodology">
<div class="eyebrow">MECHANISM</div>

<p>Three exhibits, three intuition failures. Here's the property of
randomness that's doing the work behind all of them.</p>

<p>No number in this dataset appears more or less often than chance would
predict. But that stability only shows up over the long run. Early in any
single number's history, its observed rate swings 15 to 300 times more
than it does over the full dataset — small samples are noisy by nature.
As draws accumulate, that swing narrows toward the expected rate.</p>

<p>A consecutive pair, a shared number, a long drought — each looks like
a signal because it's being read in isolation, the way a single early
swing looks like a trend before the sample catches up. Looked at across
the full history, each is part of the texture a fair random process
produces at this sample size.</p>
</div>

<hr class="rule">

<!-- ============================================================ -->
<!-- CODA — Candidate A, selected at Phase 2.5 cold-read.          -->
<!-- Candidate B retained as editorial history (not published),    -->
<!-- same treatment as Panel 2's 34.3% figure — see handoff.       -->
<!--   After three exhibits, this is exactly what you'd expect     -->
<!--   from a fair random process — patterns that don't mean       -->
<!--   anything. — Candidate B, "Resolved investigation." Rejected -->
<!--   for reading as summary rather than closure.                 -->
<!-- ============================================================ -->

<div class="coda">
Randomness isn't the absence of patterns. It's the presence of patterns
that don't mean anything.
</div>

<hr class="rule">

<!-- ============================================================ -->
<!-- SOURCES / METHODOLOGY / FOOTER — copy unchanged from Phase 3  -->
<!-- ============================================================ -->

<div class="data-sources">
<strong>Data source.</strong> NY State Gaming Commission, Powerball Winning Numbers,
2010–present. Current-era statistics (Panels 1–3) use 2015–present draws
only, matching the 2015 white-ball pool-size change.
</div>

<div class="tools-block">
<strong>Tools.</strong> R, tidyverse, ggplot2. Simulation: 5,000-replicate Monte Carlo,
real draw mechanics (5-of-69, no replacement), seed 20260702.
</div>

<div class="licence-block">
<!-- licence text carried forward from PDC template — not yet reauthored for this piece -->
</div>

<div class="footer-line">
Steven Ponce · stevenponce.netlify.app
</div>

<!-- ```{=html} -->
<!-- <script> -->
<!-- // Manual scroll-based sticky swap. -->
<!-- // Required workaround for Closeread v1.0.1 + Scrollama initialization -->
<!-- // timing issue. Scrollama fires before layout is complete and misses -->
<!-- // triggers already in the viewport on load. This script replaces -->
<!-- // Scrollama's stepEnter logic with a direct scroll listener that -->
<!-- // applies cr-active to the correct sticky on every scroll event. -->
<!-- // -->
<!-- // Logic: matches Closeread's offset: 0.5 — a trigger is "active" when -->
<!-- // its top has crossed the 50% viewport mark. The last such trigger wins. -->
<!-- // -->
<!-- // DO NOT REMOVE. DO NOT REPLACE WITH scrollama.resize() calls. -->
<!-- // Carried verbatim from PDC's 04_funafuti_tuvalu_threshold.qmd — -->
<!-- // tested there against Closeread v1.0.1, Quarto 1.6.x, Windows, Chrome. -->

<!-- window.addEventListener('load', function() { -->

<!--   var triggers = Array.from(document.querySelectorAll('.trigger[data-focus-on]')); -->
<!--   var stickies = document.querySelectorAll('.sticky'); -->

<!--   function getActiveTarget() { -->
<!--     var midpoint = window.innerHeight * 0.5; -->
<!--     var active = null; -->
<!--     triggers.forEach(function(t) { -->
<!--       var rect = t.getBoundingClientRect(); -->
<!--       if (rect.top <= midpoint) { -->
<!--         active = t.getAttribute('data-focus-on'); -->
<!--       } -->
<!--     }); -->
<!--     return active; -->
<!--   } -->

<!--   function updateStickies() { -->
<!--     var activeId = getActiveTarget(); -->
<!--     stickies.forEach(function(s) { -->
<!--       if (s.id === activeId) { -->
<!--         s.classList.add('cr-active'); -->
<!--       } else { -->
<!--         s.classList.remove('cr-active'); -->
<!--       } -->
<!--     }); -->
<!--   } -->

<!--   window.addEventListener('scroll', updateStickies); -->
<!--   setTimeout(updateStickies, 200); -->
<!-- }); -->
<!-- </script> -->
<!-- ``` -->
